Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Workday Form Failures Block Qualified Candidates

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Trent Nguyen
5mo
  • Report this post

I keep seeing job applications blocked by basic Workday form failures, such as a required university field with no selectable options. If you cannot answer it, you cannot apply. That means qualified candidates are filtered out before a human ever sees them. This is not a candidate issue. It is a UX and implementation issue, and it quietly damages recruiting pipelines and employer brand. And honestly, why pay for a system that prevents people from applying at all? If software sits between you and talent, it is part of the hiring experience. It is worth testing. Anyone else running into this? #UX #Hiring #CandidateExperience #Workday

24 31 Comments
Like Comment
Corinne de Berry 3mo
  • Report this comment

Yes, and separately, what is the point of having to attach your CV then spend ages on a Workday form inputting the same information - only to be told there are multiple, unspecified page errors that take hours to fix? Is it deliberately designed so that only the most persistent actually get to apply?

Like
Reply
3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Tamsyn Anderson 5mo
  • Report this comment

I have seen exactly this error that you're displaying here. I found a contact email for the company's HR department and wrote to them, telling them that I was trying to apply for a job but could not get past this field. Someone actually called me back, but instead of addressing the error, they told me there was no point in applying anyways because they had an internal candidate who was going to get the job. 🤣 There are so many things wrong with this story I don't even know where to start!

Reply
7 Reactions 8 Reactions
Vincent Zembruzski 4mo
  • Report this comment

Literally experienced this yesterday and figured the work around out after a very frustrating minute because I am stubborn and knew it had to be an API issue and have probably seen every permutation of these type ahead errors in one form or another and they all suck…

Yes. I've seen this with other selection lists, too.

I’ve experienced this too. When I do, I consider it their loss!

Bruno Gätjens González 5mo

Hi Trent, one thing you can try is filling out the form in Firefox; it might work. It shouldn't give those errors anyway.

1 Reaction
Leng T. 5mo
  • Report this comment

I tried to apply for Community College when I was unemployed. It asked for college info and even wants transcripts :|. I selected the submit button. It says I'm missing information but did not say what. I filled in every text box with something, even a n/a. Yet this college didn't get why no one ever applied?

3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Per Jorgensen 5mo
  • Report this comment

Oh, now you've gone and got me started on Workday again. Deep breath. Workday spawned from Beelzebub's armpit with a single mission: To make applicants prove their eagerness and resolve for *this* job for *this* particular company, which is so special that the privilege to even be considered for consideration must be earned. There, that's better now I got that off my chest. Thank you for your consideration of this matter!

Reply
4 Reactions 5 Reactions
Tera Barnes 1w
  • Report this comment

I ran into this on a major Sports industry application. Found out it was a workday site. I switched to another browser, which really helped, but by the time I could enter my University in that box, they were no longer accepting applications. Again, there is so much wrong with this!

Benjamin Schlein 5mo

Seen it myself and honestly their is one thing far worse than. Getting requests to fill out a survey on the hiring experience when you never heard back from a real person after submitting your application.

More Relevant Posts

  • Mayank Kumar
    4mo
    • Report this post

    This is beyond Workday but it’s a Must Read! ————- What happens when a skill becomes largely automatable with AI? Do those jobs just vanish? Instead of guessing, we can look at real evidence. Take customer support. AI chatbots and voice agents have been good enough to handle a majority of Tier-1 support since around 2023. That gives us a couple of years of data to observe what actually happens. Here’s what we’re seeing so far: •Headcount is mostly stable, but new hiring has slowed or frozen •The role has shifted from answering tickets to monitoring, correcting, and escalating AI responses •Ticket volume per agent has exploded •Cost per interaction has dropped, putting pressure on wages and outsourcing rates •Entry-level and freelance support roles are getting squeezed first In other words: the job didn’t disappear — it changed shape. We’re now watching the same transition begin in software, analytics, marketing, and other knowledge roles. Yes, there’s real pressure on employment. No, this is not a clean “AI replaces humans” story. In fact, many companies today have more full-time support staff than before — they’re just expected to manage far more output with AI in the loop. When the economy eventually rebounds from the current stealth slowdown and hiring resumes, we’ll likely end up with more professionals, not fewer — but each person will be amplified by AI and judged by leverage, not raw effort. The layoffs you’re about to see won’t primarily be caused by automation. They’ll be driven by macroeconomic fear, margin pressure, and delayed demand — just like 2022. AI is part of the backdrop. It’s not the executioner people think it is.

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Praveen Prakash
    5mo
    • Report this post

    WORKDAY USER EXPERIENCE! How Workday actually behaves Each company using Workday = a separate account Even though it’s the same Workday platform, accounts don’t carry over So for every new company, you usually have to: Create an account Re-enter resume details Upload resume again Answer the same questions… again 🙃 Why this happens Workday is company-specific, not candidate-centric Each employer hosts its own Workday tenant No global “Workday profile” like LinkedIn A few practical hacks (to reduce pain) Keep a master resume in DOCX (Workday parses DOCX better than PDF) Maintain a single text file with: Role descriptions Skills Summary → copy-paste quickly when Workday fails parsing Use same email + password pattern for all Workday accounts (password manager helps a lot) Apply first via: LinkedIn / referral → sometimes skips some steps #workday #jobs #jobapply #jobseekers #ux #itjobs #linkedin #ai #resumes #recruiters

    4 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Michael Skarzynski
    4mo
    • Report this post

    Workday Layoffs in 2026: Why Customer Operations is Feeling the Burn By Diana Coker Silicon Valley is hitting a rough patch early this year, and the latest news from Pleasanton has everyone talking. As we track the wave of layoffs in 2026, Workday has just confirmed a fresh round of workday job cuts in 2026 that specifically target their Global Customer Operations. Workday layoffs aren’t just a rounding error, they represent a calculated shift in how the SaaS giant intends to handle its post-sales lifecycle moving forward. For those of us in the HR space, this feels quite personal. When a platform that manages your people starts cutting its own people, you have to look at the “why” behind the move. The grim reality of the Workday Layoffs in 2026 In a recent filing, Workday detailed plans to eliminate roughly 400 positions, or about 2% of its total workforce. While that might sound modest compared to the massive layoffs in 2026, we’ve seen at other big-tech firms, the “who” is more important than the “how many.” It looks a targeted strike on the teams that help customers actually use the software. The company is positioning these Workday layoffs in 2026 as a way to “realign” resources away from HR support and toward revenue-generating sales and AI development. It leaves many wondering if the “customer-first” culture is being traded for “margin-first” metrics. Behind the Workday job cuts With Carl Eschenbach stepping down and co-founder Aneel Bhusri returning to the driver’s seat, the company is clearly in a state of flux. Investors are demanding higher GAAP operating margins, and job cuts in 2026 are the quickest way to balance the books before the fiscal year-end reports. The restructuring is expected to be wrapped up by April 2026, but the shadow it casts on the company’s culture is significant. When you cut the Global Customer Operations team, you’re essentially betting that AI and self-service portals can replace the nuance of human account management. Layoffs in 2026 aren’t happening because these companies are failing but rather because the goalposts have moved. The obsession with “efficiency” has replaced the “growth at all costs” mentality of the previous decade. Workday will cut the “overhead” (human support) and double down on the “innovation” (automation). Workday job cuts are a warning to other legacy SaaS providers that aren’t yet lean enough to compete in an AI-dominated landscape. If you’re a Workday customer, these workday layoffs might mean a change in your support experience. “High-Touch” service is becoming a premium add-on rather than a standard feature. As the workday layoffs in 2026 take effect, expect a push toward more automated troubleshooting and fewer dedicated AEs. Workday job cuts signal that the company is bracing for a leaner future. Whether the same level of customer care is possible remains to be seen. https://lnkd.in/dNuvPBgY

    Workday Laoyffs in 2026: Why Customer Operations is Feeling the Burn https://www.thehrdigest.com

    𝗩𝗶𝗿𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗛𝗶𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝘀 & 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘄𝘁𝗵 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸 (𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟲–𝟮𝟬𝟯𝟰) 🔹 The Virtual Hiring Tools Market was valued at USD 2.44 Billion in 2025 and is expected to grow from USD 2.75 Billion in 2026 to USD 7.07 Billion by 2034, registering a CAGR of 12.54% during 2026–2034. This growth reflects the rising adoption of digital recruitment platforms, AI-driven hiring, and remote workforce strategies across industries. Here is the detailed Insights: https://lnkd.in/guwHm_xR 🔹 𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗗𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀 • Growing demand for remote and hybrid hiring models • Increasing use of AI and automation in recruitment processes • Rising focus on reducing time-to-hire and hiring costs • Expansion of cloud-based HR and talent management solutions • Need for scalable hiring tools among fast-growing enterprises 🔹 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗦𝗲𝗴𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 ➤ By Component: Software | Services ➤ By Application: Recruitment | Onboarding | Training | Others ➤ By Deployment Mode: On-Premises | Cloud ➤ By Enterprise Size: Small & Medium Enterprises | Large Enterprises ➤ By End-User: BFSI | Healthcare | IT & Telecommunications | Retail | Manufacturing | Others 🔹 𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿𝘀 • LinkedIn • Indeed • Workday • ADP • SAP SuccessFactors • Oracle • Glassdoor • ZipRecruiter • Monster • CareerBuilder • SmartRecruiters • iCIMS • Greenhouse • BambooHR • Bullhorn #VirtualHiring #HiringTechnology #RecruitmentSoftware #HRTech #TalentAcquisition #DigitalRecruitment #FutureOfWork #AIinHiring #CloudHR #WorkforceManagement #HiringTrends

  • Gavin McLean
    4mo
    • Report this post

    My heart reaches out to everyone affected by the Workday layoffs ❤️🩹. While the headlines focus on "efficiency" and "AI-driven restructuring", we cannot forget the 400 talented individuals, many in customer-facing roles, who are now navigating an uncertain weekend. Customer support is often labelled “non-revenue generating”, yet it is usually where customers feel the brand most directly. While AI will absolutely change how these roles operate, we know that efficiency is never neutral. There are real people behind every restructure, and the conversation we need to be having as an industry is not: “Can AI replace this role?” but: “How do we redesign work so humans are elevated, not erased?” AI should remove friction, not dignity. To those impacted: Please know that your value is not defined by a regulatory filing. Your skills in problem-solving, your ability to advocate for users, and your dedication to the community are exactly what make organisations thrive. To my network: If there are any open roles in your organisation, especially in Customer Operations, Product, Success, or Programme Management, now is the time to share. That is exactly what LinkedIn and this community are for: being the bridge for world-class talent when the ground shifts. Let's do our part to help these people find their next great chapter as quickly as possible. 🤝 #TitanBrands #CommunitySupport #Hiring #TechLayoffs #PayItForward #Workday

    Workday Feb 24 Earnings; 400 Layoffs, AI Expansion: Workday set its fiscal Q4 FY26 results for Feb. 24 and announced roughly 400 job cuts to refocus on AI and high-priority initiatives. This article summarizes near-term catalysts, financial implica… #stockmarket #wday

  • Brandon Dohman
    4mo
    • Report this post

    One of the biggest user-experience conundrums on the planet has fascinated me for many years. We're many... many years into this issue, and no one has fixed it. Sure, there are some hacky Chrome plugins and auto-apply apps (generally a waste of space, and I don't recommend them) Workday, why do you make it an unbearable process for applicants to apply to jobs? At this point, I feel like it's just a sick experiment in human torture. For those of you who haven't experienced this user experience nightmare, let me walk you through what modern-day candidates go through... Candidate: Surfs LinkedIn looking for jobs. Finds one they are really excited about. They prep their resume, maybe write one of those awful cover letters some companies still ask for, and then get into the application process. Step 1: Create a Workday account for {Company A} Step 2: Upload your resume, have it parsed horribly, fix it all, and proceed to fill out the application. Step 3: Review all the data provided, notice a parsing error in the resume, go back and fix it. Step 4: Nervously click submit because you feel like you've missed something. Candidate: Goes back to LinkedIn, finds another job they want to apply for. Tweaks their resume for that job Step 1: CREATE A NEW WORKDAY ACCOUNT FOR {COMPANY B} STEP 2: SAME AS ABOVE STEP 3: SAMESIES AGAIN STEP 4: AGAIN... SAMESIES I could repeat this over and over. Candidate: Eventually resents Workday. Candidate: Finally gets a job, becomes sad when they have to use Workday again. This is why I'm such a huge proponent of human-centered design and design thinking. It puts the human first. Things like this likely don't go unnoticed when you take the time to create empathy with your users. HINT: The user isn't just the corporation you're selling your software to. This could EASILY be fixed by creating an experience for the applicant. Give them a way to track their applications across all the Workday sites they have applied through. Give them an easy way to customize their resumes for applications. It's not just the right thing to do for humanity's sake, but imagine how many new advocates you could create if you did this...? Rather than the current experience of - I HATE WORKDAY. #SundayThoughts #SNLHomage

    17 7 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Jill Carter
    5mo
    • Report this post

    This is an important conversation. I’ve worked on AI systems and I’ve also been on the other side as an applicant going through Workday-powered pipelines. One thing is clear: when hiring becomes mediated by AI + automation, the biggest risk isn’t just bias — it’s lack of visibility. If a candidate is rejected instantly, they can’t tell whether it was: - a rule-based knockout filter - a resume parsing failure - an internal candidate scenario - a threshold model decision - or something else entirely In any other AI-enabled domain, we’d demand monitoring and auditability. Hiring should be no different.

    Maureen Wiley Clough

    Corporate Dropout Fighting for People Over 40 at Work | Millennial Crashout | Seen in Forbes, Business Insider & WIRED | Podcaster | Speaker | Creator | Writer | Future Author | 100M Views ’24-’25

    5mo Edited

    If you're over 40 and wondering why you keep getting rejected for jobs you're very qualified for, this might be why. Derek Mobley says he got rejected 100+ times by companies using Workday's AI hiring system. He claims many rejections came minutes after hitting "apply" - even in the middle of the night. He's over 40. He saw a pattern, believed it was age discrimination, and sued. A federal judge just allowed his case to move forward as a class action. The potential scale is wild: 65% of Fortune 500 companies use Workday Active in 175 countries Hundreds of millions potentially impacted NOTE: Workday denies these allegations, and nothing's been proven yet. But regardless of what happens with this case, here's what gives me hope: one person refused to accept constant rejection as normal. And now we might see the biggest hiring discrimination case ever. Your reminder: Those rejections aren't about your skills - they're about a hot trash job market You're not "too old" - the system is broken Your experience is valuable, not a liability ACTION: If you were over 40 and applied to jobs after September 2020 on Workday, you may be able to join the class. DEADLINE: March 7th at workdaycase.com More details courtesy of Sheila Callaham: https://lnkd.in/gqpvsfsq PS. if you want more updates on the news and issues impacting people in mid-career, hit up itgetslateearly.com for the newsletter and episodes! #ageism #hiring

    11
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Amanda Peranzi, SHRM-CP
    5mo
    • Report this post

    300+ rejections. June through December. I just lived this! As an 40+ HR professional now working on a Workday implementation, I can tell you the instant rejections aren't necessarily about age - they're usually knockout questions or missing keywords. But here's the thing: that doesn't make it less broken. These AI screening tools are only as good as how they're configured. They're trained on historical data that often reflects existing biases. Companies implement them as a shortcut to avoid the harder work of truly evaluating candidates. And when you're on the receiving end - getting rejected within hours when you KNOW you can do the job - it's soul-crushing. Whether it's age discrimination, bad filtering, or poorly configured algorithms, the system needs serious scrutiny. Companies should be auditing their hiring tech regularly, not just implementing and forgetting. My 16+ years of HR experience should be an asset, not something filtered out because I don't match some algorithm's narrow criteria.

    Maureen Wiley Clough

    Corporate Dropout Fighting for People Over 40 at Work | Millennial Crashout | Seen in Forbes, Business Insider & WIRED | Podcaster | Speaker | Creator | Writer | Future Author | 100M Views ’24-’25

    5mo Edited

    If you're over 40 and wondering why you keep getting rejected for jobs you're very qualified for, this might be why. Derek Mobley says he got rejected 100+ times by companies using Workday's AI hiring system. He claims many rejections came minutes after hitting "apply" - even in the middle of the night. He's over 40. He saw a pattern, believed it was age discrimination, and sued. A federal judge just allowed his case to move forward as a class action. The potential scale is wild: 65% of Fortune 500 companies use Workday Active in 175 countries Hundreds of millions potentially impacted NOTE: Workday denies these allegations, and nothing's been proven yet. But regardless of what happens with this case, here's what gives me hope: one person refused to accept constant rejection as normal. And now we might see the biggest hiring discrimination case ever. Your reminder: Those rejections aren't about your skills - they're about a hot trash job market You're not "too old" - the system is broken Your experience is valuable, not a liability ACTION: If you were over 40 and applied to jobs after September 2020 on Workday, you may be able to join the class. DEADLINE: March 7th at workdaycase.com More details courtesy of Sheila Callaham: https://lnkd.in/gqpvsfsq PS. if you want more updates on the news and issues impacting people in mid-career, hit up itgetslateearly.com for the newsletter and episodes! #ageism #hiring

    3 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Natalie M. Canfield, MS
    5mo
    • Report this post

    Last week, I recieved an automated rejection for a job I’m distinctly qualified for at an organization where I’ve worked and have a vast network. My initial suspicion is that the ATS system noted my years of experience and rejected my materials. Yet, as an applicant, I have no insight. There is no transparency and it’s hard to know if discrimination is the culprit. It’s disheartening that in the age of AI, it’s going to get harder and harder to treat people like humans. These systems are bombarded with thousands of applications and it is impossible for a person to manually sort through them all. So- computational methods absolutely have to remain part of the process. Regardless, ageism should not be programmed into any ATS system. Your system should be looking for a floor, not a ceiling. Candidates need to meet minimum requirements- if your system eliminates people for too much experience, your system is being discriminatory. Additionally, there used to be professionalism about how we treat candidates. For example, if someone gets a personal recommendation from within the organization, they’d get a phone interview at the very least. I fear professionalism has been lost in the over-reliance on these systems, and that a lot of talented folks are being looked over. Of course, there could be many other reasons for the rejection I just recieved. In the age of AI, I believe that organizations that meet people with humanity as much as possible are going to be the ones that keep humanity intact as the age of AI envelopes us all. 💛

    Maureen Wiley Clough

    Corporate Dropout Fighting for People Over 40 at Work | Millennial Crashout | Seen in Forbes, Business Insider & WIRED | Podcaster | Speaker | Creator | Writer | Future Author | 100M Views ’24-’25

    5mo Edited

    If you're over 40 and wondering why you keep getting rejected for jobs you're very qualified for, this might be why. Derek Mobley says he got rejected 100+ times by companies using Workday's AI hiring system. He claims many rejections came minutes after hitting "apply" - even in the middle of the night. He's over 40. He saw a pattern, believed it was age discrimination, and sued. A federal judge just allowed his case to move forward as a class action. The potential scale is wild: 65% of Fortune 500 companies use Workday Active in 175 countries Hundreds of millions potentially impacted NOTE: Workday denies these allegations, and nothing's been proven yet. But regardless of what happens with this case, here's what gives me hope: one person refused to accept constant rejection as normal. And now we might see the biggest hiring discrimination case ever. Your reminder: Those rejections aren't about your skills - they're about a hot trash job market You're not "too old" - the system is broken Your experience is valuable, not a liability ACTION: If you were over 40 and applied to jobs after September 2020 on Workday, you may be able to join the class. DEADLINE: March 7th at workdaycase.com More details courtesy of Sheila Callaham: https://lnkd.in/gqpvsfsq PS. if you want more updates on the news and issues impacting people in mid-career, hit up itgetslateearly.com for the newsletter and episodes! #ageism #hiring
